1978.         <member name="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient">
1979.             <summary>Represents a chat client.</summary>
1980.             <remarks>
1981.             <para>
1982.             Applications must consider risks such as prompt injection attacks, data sizes, and the number of messages
1983.             sent to the underlying provider or returned from it. Unless a specific <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> implementation
1984.             explicitly documents safeguards for these concerns, the application is expected to implement appropriate protections.
1985.             </para>
1986.             <para>
1987.             Unless otherwise specified, all members of <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> are thread-safe for concurrent use.
1988.             It is expected that all implementations of <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> support being used by multiple requests concurrently.
1989.             Instances must not be disposed of while the instance is still in use.
1990.             </para>
1991.             <para>
1992.             However, implementations of <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> might mutate the arguments supplied to <see cref="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetResponseAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage},Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken)"/> and
1993.             <see cref="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetStreamingResponseAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage},Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken)"/>, such as by configuring the options instance. Thus, consumers of the interface either
1994.             should avoid using shared instances of these arguments for concurrent invocations or should otherwise ensure by construction
1995.             that no <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> instances are used which might employ such mutation. For example, the ConfigureOptions method is
1996.             provided with a callback that could mutate the supplied options argument, and that should be avoided if using a singleton options instance.
1997.             </para>
1998.             </remarks>
1999.             <related type="Article" href="https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/quickstarts/build-chat-app">Build an AI chat app with .NET.</related>
2000.             <related type="Article" href="https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai#the-ichatclient-interface">The IChatClient interface.</related>
2001.         </member>
2002.         <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetResponseAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage},Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken)">
2003.             <summary>Sends chat messages and returns the response.</summary>
2004.             <param name="messages">The sequence of chat messages to send.</param>
2005.             <param name="options">The chat options with which to configure the request.</param>
2006.             <param name="cancellationToken">The <see cref="T:System.Threading.CancellationToken"/> to monitor for cancellation requests. The default is <see cref="P:System.Threading.CancellationToken.None"/>.</param>
2007.             <returns>The response messages generated by the client.</returns>
2008.             <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentNullException"><paramref name="messages"/> is <see langword="null"/>.</exception>
2009.             <related type="Article" href="https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai#request-a-chat-response">Request a chat response.</related>
2010.         </member>
2011.         <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetStreamingResponseAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage},Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken)">
2012.             <summary>Sends chat messages and streams the response.</summary>
2013.             <param name="messages">The sequence of chat messages to send.</param>
2014.             <param name="options">The chat options with which to configure the request.</param>
2015.             <param name="cancellationToken">The <see cref="T:System.Threading.CancellationToken"/> to monitor for cancellation requests. The default is <see cref="P:System.Threading.CancellationToken.None"/>.</param>
2016.             <returns>The response messages generated by the client.</returns>
2017.             <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentNullException"><paramref name="messages"/> is <see langword="null"/>.</exception>
2018.             <related type="Article" href="https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai#request-a-streaming-chat-response">Request a streaming chat response.</related>
2019.         </member>
2020.         <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetService(System.Type,System.Object)">
2021.             <summary>Asks the <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> for an object of the specified type <paramref name="serviceType"/>.</summary>
2022.             <param name="serviceType">The type of object being requested.</param>
2023.             <param name="serviceKey">An optional key that can be used to help identify the target service.</param>
2024.             <returns>The found object, otherwise <see langword="null"/>.</returns>
2025.             <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentNullException"><paramref name="serviceType"/> is <see langword="null"/>.</exception>
2026.             <remarks>
2027.             The purpose of this method is to allow for the retrieval of strongly typed services that might be provided by the <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/>,
2028.             including itself or any services it might be wrapping. For example, to access the <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatClientMetadata"/> for the instance,
2029.             <see cref="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetService(System.Type,System.Object)"/> may be used to request it.
2030.             </remarks>
2031.         </member>
2032.         <member name="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode">
2033.             <summary>
2034.             Indicates that an <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> should not request the invocation of any tools.
2035.             </summary>
2036.             <remarks>
2037.             Use <see cref="P:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatToolMode.None"/> to get an instance of <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode"/>.
2038.             </remarks>
2039.         </member>
2040.         <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode.#ctor">
2041.             <summary>Initializes a new instance of the <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode"/> class.</summary>
2042.             <remarks>Use <see cref="P:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatToolMode.None"/> to get an instance of <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode"/>.</remarks>
2043.         </member>
2044.         <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode.Equals(System.Object)">
2045.             <inheritdoc/>
2046.         </member>
2047.         <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.NoneChatToolMode.GetHashCode">
2048.             <inheritdoc/>
2049.         </member>
2050.         <member name="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningEffort">
2051.             <summary>
2052.             Specifies the level of reasoning effort that should be applied when generating chat responses.
2053.             </summary>
2054.             <remarks>
2055.             This value suggests how much computational effort the model should put into reasoning.
2056.             Higher values may result in more thoughtful responses but with increased latency and token usage.
2057.             The specific interpretation and support for each level may vary between providers or even between models from the same provider.
2058.             </remarks>
2059.         </member>
2060.         <member name="F:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningEffort.None">
2061.             <summary>
2062.             No reasoning effort.
2063.             </summary>
2064.         </member>
2065.         <member name="F:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningEffort.Low">
2066.             <summary>
2067.             Low reasoning effort. Minimal reasoning for faster responses.
2068.             </summary>
2069.         </member>
2070.         <member name="F:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningEffort.Medium">
2071.             <summary>
2072.             Medium reasoning effort. Balanced reasoning for most use cases.
2073.             </summary>
2074.         </member>
2075.         <member name="F:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningEffort.High">
2076.             <summary>
2077.             High reasoning effort. Extensive reasoning for complex tasks.
2078.             </summary>
2079.         </member>
2080.         <member name="F:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningEffort.ExtraHigh">
2081.             <summary>
2082.             Extra high reasoning effort. Maximum reasoning for the most demanding tasks.
2083.             </summary>
2084.         </member>
2085.         <member name="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ReasoningOptions">
2086.             <summary>
2087.             Represents options for configuring reasoning behavior in chat requests.
2088.             </summary>
2089.             <remarks>
2090.             <para>
2091.             Reasoning options allow control over how much computational effort the model
2092.             should put into reasoning about the response, and how that reasoning should
2093.             be exposed to the caller.
2094.             </para>
2095.             <para>
2096.             Not all providers support all reasoning options. Implementations should
2097.             make a best-effort attempt to map the requested options to the provider's
2098.             capabilities. If a provider or model doesn't support reasoning or doesn't support the requested configuration of reasoning, these options may be ignored.
2099.             </para>
2100.             </remarks>

## **Microsoft.Agents.AI NuGet Package API Surface Analysis**

### **Package Versions Restored**

---

## **Key Classes and Abstractions**

### **1. Core Agent Framework (Microsoft.Agents.AI.Abstractions)**

#### **`Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent`** (abstract base class)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Agents.AI`

**Core Methods for Invoking Agents**:
```csharp
// Multiple RunAsync overloads:
Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
    IEnumerable<ChatMessage> messages, 
    AgentSession session, 
    AgentRunOptions options, 
    CancellationToken cancellationToken)

Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
    string message,  // text wrapped as ChatMessage with User role
    AgentSession session,
    AgentRunOptions options,
    CancellationToken cancellationToken)

Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
    ChatMessage message,
    AgentSession session,
    AgentRunOptions options,
    CancellationToken cancellationToken)

Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
    AgentSession session,
    AgentRunOptions options,
    CancellationToken cancellationToken)

// Streaming variants (return IAsyncEnumerable<AgentResponseUpdate>):
IAsyncEnumerable<AgentResponseUpdate> RunStreamingAsync(...)
```

**Key Properties**:
- `Id`: Unique identifier for agent instance
- `Name`: Human-readable name
- `Description`: Description of agent's purpose/capabilities
- `CurrentRunContext`: AgentRunContext for current agent run (flows across async calls)

**Session Management**:
```csharp
Task<AgentSession> CreateSessionAsync(CancellationToken)
Task<JsonElement> SerializeSessionAsync(AgentSession, JsonSerializerOptions, CancellationToken)
Task<AgentSession> DeserializeSessionAsync(JsonElement, JsonSerializerOptions, CancellationToken)
```

---

#### **`Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Agents.AI`

**Core Properties**:
- `ContinuationToken`: Token for resuming background responses
- `AllowBackgroundResponses`: Enable async background operations
- `ResponseFormat`: `ChatResponseFormat` (Text/Json)
- `AdditionalProperties`: Custom metadata dictionary

**Method**:
```csharp
AgentRunOptions Clone()
```

---

#### **`Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentSession`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Agents.AI`

Represents conversation state that may include:
- Conversation history
- External history references
- Memories/state
- Custom behaviors for storage, data extraction, chat reduction

---

### **2. Chat Client Agent Implementation (Microsoft.Agents.AI)**

#### **`Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent`** (implements AIAgent)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Agents.AI`

**Constructors**:
```csharp
// Simple constructor with individual parameters:
ChatClientAgent(
    IChatClient chatClient,
    string instructions,           // optional system instructions
    string name,                   // optional agent name
    string description,            // optional description
    IList<AITool> tools,          // optional tools collection
    ILoggerFactory loggerFactory, // optional
    IServiceProvider services)    // optional for DI

// Options-based constructor:
ChatClientAgent(
    IChatClient chatClient,
    ChatClientAgentOptions options,
    ILoggerFactory loggerFactory,
    IServiceProvider services)
```

**Key Properties**:
- `ChatClient`: The underlying IChatClient instance (may be decorated pipeline)
- `ChatHistoryProvider`: Provider for chat history (if not server-stored)
- `AIContextProviders`: List of AIContextProvider instances for additional context
- `Instructions`: System instructions guiding agent behavior
- `ChatOptions`: Default chat options used by agent

**Core Invoke Methods**: (inherited from AIAgent)
```csharp
Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(...)
IAsyncEnumerable<AgentResponseUpdate> RunStreamingAsync(...)
```

---

#### **`Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions`** (extends AgentRunOptions)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Agents.AI`

**Additional Properties**:

- `ChatClientFactory`: `Func<IChatClient, IChatClient>` to replace/decorate chat client per-request

---

#### **`Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentSession`** (implements AgentSession)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Agents.AI`

**Properties**:

**Methods**:
```csharp
static ChatClientAgentSession Deserialize(JsonElement, JsonSerializerOptions)
JsonElement Serialize(JsonSerializerOptions)
```

---

### **3. Microsoft.Extensions.AI Core Abstractions**

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient`** (interface)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

**Core Methods**:
```csharp

    IEnumerable<ChatMessage> messages,
    ChatOptions options,
    CancellationToken cancellationToken)

```

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

**Properties**:
```csharp
string ConversationId              // associate with existing conversation
string Instructions                // per-request system instructions
double? Temperature                // randomness control
int? MaxOutputTokens              // token limit
double? TopP                       // nucleus sampling
int? TopK                          // top-K tokens
double? FrequencyPenalty          // repeated token penalty
double? PresencePenalty           // token existence penalty
int? Seed                          // reproducibility seed
ReasoningOptions Reasoning         // reasoning configuration
ChatResponseFormat ResponseFormat  // Text/Json format
string ModelId                     // model identifier
IList<string> StopSequences       // stop generation sequences
bool? AllowMultipleToolCalls      // allow multiple tool calls per response
ChatToolMode ToolMode             // tool calling mode (Auto/None/Required)
IList<AITool> Tools               // tools available to LLM
bool? AllowBackgroundResponses    // allow async background operations
```

**Constructor**:
```csharp
ChatOptions()
ChatOptions(ChatOptions other)  // shallow copy
```

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage`**

Represents a chat message with:

- `Content`: IList<AIContent>

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatResponse`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

**Properties**:
- `Choices`: List of chat completions
- `Usage`: Token usage information
- `ConversationId`: Associated conversation ID
- `ModelId`: Model used
- Additional metadata

### **4. Tool/Function Definitions (Microsoft.Extensions.AI)**

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AITool`** (base class)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

Base abstraction for tools; subclasses include `AIFunction` and `AIFunctionDeclaration`.

---

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunction`** (extends AIFunctionDeclaration)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

**Properties**:
- `UnderlyingMethod`: `MethodInfo` if wrapping a .NET method
- `JsonSerializerOptions`: For marshaling function parameters

**Key Methods**:
```csharp
Task<object> InvokeAsync(
    AIFunctionArguments arguments,
    CancellationToken cancellationToken)

AIFunctionDeclaration AsDeclarationOnly()  // create non-invokable description
```

---

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionFactory`** (static factory)
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

**Static Methods**:
```csharp
// Create from delegate:
static AIFunction Create(
    Delegate method,
    AIFunctionFactoryOptions options)

static AIFunction Create(
    Delegate method,
    string name,
    string description,
    JsonSerializerOptions serializerOptions)

// Create from MethodInfo:
static AIFunction Create(
    MethodInfo method,
    object target,                    // null for static methods
    AIFunctionFactoryOptions options)
```

**Features**:
- Automatically derives JSON schemas for parameters
- Handles CancellationToken, IServiceProvider, AIFunctionArguments specially
- Marshals parameters from dictionary
- Serializes return values (except AIContent types)
- Derives return JSON schema

---

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionArguments`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

Dictionary-like container for function arguments:
```csharp
// Implements IDictionary<string, object>
AIFunctionArguments()
AIFunctionArguments(IDictionary<string, object> arguments)
AIFunctionArguments(IEqualityComparer<string> comparer)

IServiceProvider Services { get; set; }      // for DI resolution
IDictionary<string, object> Context { get; set; }  // custom context
```

---

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionFactoryOptions`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

**Key Properties**:
- `SerializerOptions`: `JsonSerializerOptions` for marshaling
- `JsonSchemaCreateOptions`: Schema generation configuration
- `MarshalResult`: Custom return value marshaling delegate
- `ExcludeResultSchema`: Skip return schema generation
- `ConfigureParameterBinding`: Custom parameter binding logic

---

### **5. Function Invoking Middleware (Microsoft.Extensions.AI)**

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

Decorates IChatClient to automatically:
- Invoke tool calls made by LLM
- Handle function results
- Loop until no more tool calls or max iterations reached

**Inner Types**:
- `FunctionInvocationStatus`: Success/Failure/Rejected
- `FunctionInvocationResult`: Result of a function call

---

## **Typical Usage Flow**

### **Step 1: Create IChatClient**
```csharp
// From OpenAI or other provider
IChatClient chatClient = new OpenAIClient(...).AsChatClient();
```

### **Step 2: Define Tools as AIFunction**
```csharp
// From methods using AIFunctionFactory
var tools = new List<AITool>
{
    AIFunctionFactory.Create((string query) => DoSomething(query)),
    AIFunctionFactory.Create(SomeMethod),
};
```

### **Step 3: Create ChatClientAgent**
```csharp
var agent = new ChatClientAgent(
    chatClient,
    instructions: "You are a helpful assistant",
    name: "MyAgent",
    description: "Handles user queries",
    tools: tools);
```

### **Step 4: Create Session**
```csharp
var session = await agent.CreateSessionAsync(cancellationToken);
```

### **Step 5: Invoke Agent**
```csharp
// With RunAsync (collects all responses):
var response = await agent.RunAsync(
    "User message here",
    session,
    new ChatClientAgentRunOptions(
        new ChatOptions
        {
            MaxOutputTokens = 1000,
            Temperature = 0.7
        }),
    cancellationToken);

// Or with RunStreamingAsync (streams responses):
await foreach (var update in agent.RunStreamingAsync(
    "User message",
    session,
    new ChatClientAgentRunOptions(),
    cancellationToken))
{
    // Process update
}
```

### **Step 6: Access Response**
```csharp
// response is AgentResponse
var text = response.Choices[0].Content;  // The agent's text response
```

---

## **Key Abstractions Summary**

| **What** | **Where** | **How** |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Create agent from IChatClient | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent` | Constructor with IChatClient + options |
| Register tools | `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions.Tools` | Create AIFunction via AIFunctionFactory, add to list |
| Invoke agent | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent.RunAsync()` | Call with messages, session, run options |
| Stream responses | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent.RunStreamingAsync()` | Async enumerate over AgentResponseUpdate |
| Tool calling | `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient` | Middleware that auto-invokes tools |
| Iteration control | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions` | Set via ChatClientAgentRunOptions |

### **5. Function Invoking Middleware (Microsoft.Extensions.AI)**

#### **`Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient`**
**Namespace**: `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`

Decorates IChatClient to automatically:
- Invoke tool calls made by LLM
- Handle function results
- Loop until no more tool calls or max iterations reached

**Inner Types**:
- `FunctionInvocationStatus`: Success/Failure/Rejected
- `FunctionInvocationResult`: Result of a function call

---

## **Typical Usage Flow**

### **Step 1: Create IChatClient**
```csharp
// From OpenAI or other provider
IChatClient chatClient = new OpenAIClient(...).AsChatClient();
```

### **Step 2: Define Tools as AIFunction**
```csharp
// From methods using AIFunctionFactory
var tools = new List<AITool>
{
    AIFunctionFactory.Create((string query) => DoSomething(query)),
    AIFunctionFactory.Create(SomeMethod),
};
```

### **Step 3: Create ChatClientAgent**
```csharp
var agent = new ChatClientAgent(
    chatClient,
    instructions: "You are a helpful assistant",
    name: "MyAgent",
    description: "Handles user queries",
    tools: tools);
```

### **Step 4: Create Session**
```csharp
var session = await agent.CreateSessionAsync(cancellationToken);
```

### **Step 5: Invoke Agent**
```csharp
// With RunAsync (collects all responses):
var response = await agent.RunAsync(
    "User message here",
    session,
    new ChatClientAgentRunOptions(
        new ChatOptions
        {
            MaxOutputTokens = 1000,
            Temperature = 0.7
        }),
    cancellationToken);

// Or with RunStreamingAsync (streams responses):
await foreach (var update in agent.RunStreamingAsync(
    "User message",
    session,
    new ChatClientAgentRunOptions(),
    cancellationToken))
{
    // Process update
}
```

### **Step 6: Access Response**
```csharp
// response is AgentResponse
var text = response.Choices[0].Content;  // The agent's text response
```

---

## **Key Abstractions Summary**

| **What** | **Where** | **How** |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Create agent from IChatClient | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent` | Constructor with IChatClient + options |
| Register tools | `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions.Tools` | Create AIFunction via AIFunctionFactory, add to list |
| Invoke agent | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent.RunAsync()` | Call with messages, session, run options |
| Stream responses | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent.RunStreamingAsync()` | Async enumerate over AgentResponseUpdate |
| Tool calling | `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient` | Middleware that auto-invokes tools |
| Iteration control | `Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions` | Set via ChatClientAgentRunOptions |

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the API surface. Let me build the research agent app. Here's my plan:

1. **Program.cs** - Main entry point with DI setup, agent creation, and execution
2. **Tools/WebSearchTool.cs** - Web search tool using HttpClient to fetch web pages
3. **Tools/NoteTakingTool.cs** - Note-taking tool to store and retrieve research notes

Key design decisions:
- Use `ChatClientAgent` from `Microsoft.Agents.AI`
- Define tools via `AIFunctionFactory.Create`
- Use `FunctionInvokingChatClient` middleware for auto tool dispatch


- Pull the API key from environment variables and configure iteration limits through ChatOptions with temperature set to 0 for factual research

Looking at the agent API more carefully, `ChatClientAgent` takes the chat client, instructions, name, description, and a list of tools in its constructor. The tools are created as `AITool` instances through `AIFunctionFactory.Create`, and I'll invoke the agent using `RunAsync` or `RunStreamingAsync` depending on whether I want streaming responses.

For the web search tool, I need to decide on the implementation approach. Since we don't have real search API credentials, I'll create a tool that makes HTTP requests to fetch actual web content—that's more practical for a demo that can actually run without external dependencies.

I'm planning two main tools: one that searches the web using a simple HTTP approach, and another that fetches specific page content. For the note-taking functionality, I'll add methods to store and retrieve research notes. Now I need to figure out how the ChatClientAgent handles tool invocation internally.

Looking at the framework, I think the key decision is whether to wrap the IChatClient with FunctionInvokingChatClient as middleware, or pass the tools directly to ChatClientAgent and let it manage the orchestration loop. Both approaches seem viable, so I need to determine which one aligns better with how the agent framework is designed to work. Client`, the tools would be invoked automatically by the middleware, so I might not need to pass them separately to `ChatClientAgent`. Let me think through the flow: if `FunctionInvokingChatClient` handles the tool invocation loop, then `ChatClientAgent` just needs the wrapped client. But the agent still needs to know about the tools for context. I'll pass the tools to both the middleware and the agent to be safe. I think the `ChatClientAgent` constructor accepts tools directly, so I should pass them there rather than trying to wire them up separately. The agent framework should handle the tool invocation internally, so I'll go with passing tools to the agent constructor and let it manage the dispatch.

Setting up the implementation... Setting up the agent session and running it with the research topic, then I'm checking whether the `AsIChatClient()` method is available on the OpenAI ChatClient from the Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI package. I'm planning the file structure for the console app with separate files for the web search tool and note-taking functionality, then I'll implement the web search using HttpClient to fetch and parse HTML content from a search engine.

Writing WebSearchTool implementation... Finishing up the search function with error handling, then moving on to define a page fetching method that retrieves HTML content from a URL and truncates it if it exceeds a certain length.

Now I'm thinking through the HTML parsing logic more carefully—DuckDuckGo results have a specific structure, so I need regex patterns to extract the snippet and title elements properly. I'm also reconsidering how the ChatClientAgent and FunctionInvokingChatClient work together, particularly whether the agent uses the invoking client internally when tools are passed to its constructor.

The flow seems to be that when the LLM returns a tool call, the FunctionInvokingChatClient intercepts it, invokes the actual tool, and sends the result back to the LLM, which then continues reasoning until it produces a final response. The ChatClientAgent sits on top of this, managing the conversation history. So my approach should be to wrap the base OpenAI client with the FunctionInvokingChatClient middleware, then pass the tools through ChatOptions so the LLM knows what's available, and let the invoking client handle the actual tool dispatch. I'm checking the structure of `AgentResponse` to understand what properties it exposes for retrieving the agent's output, then I'll write out the complete implementation and iterate through any compilation issues.

Setting up the Program.cs file with the OpenAI client, chat client builder with function invocation enabled, and initializing the research notes and tools list... Setting the topic to the latest quantum computing advances, then creating an agent session and running it with streaming output to display the research results in real-time. I'm checking whether `ChatClientAgentRunOptions` accepts `ChatOptions` as a constructor parameter or if it needs to be set as a property instead. Writing the ResearchNotes class... Writing the web search implementation... Extracting search results... I'm checking the structure of `ChatClientAgentRunOptions` to understand its constructor and properties, particularly the `ChatOptions` and `ChatClientFactory` fields, so I can properly instantiate it with an object initializer. I also need to verify what properties `AgentResponseUpdate` exposes for the streaming response handling. So the pattern is either creating a new `ChatClientBuilder` with the inner client, or calling `.AsBuilder()` on the client itself, then chaining `.UseFunctionInvocation()` before calling `.Build()` to get the final middleware-wrapped client. Let me just start writing the code and work through any compilation errors as they come up. I also need to figure out what the `update.Text` property looks like.

Looking at the package versions, I'm seeing `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` version `10.5.1` in the explore results, but the skill guide mentions version `9.*`, and the csproj is pinned to `9.*`. Let me check what actually got restored.

I think `Microsoft.Agents.AI 1.5.0` might have a dependency on `Microsoft.Extensions.AI 10.x`, which could be causing a version conflict. Rather than debugging this further, I'll just write out all the files I need and attempt to build, then fix any issues that come up.

I need to create or update the project files: the csproj, Program.cs, WebSearchTool.cs, and ResearchNotes.cs. Let me start writing these now.

Setting up the Program.cs file... Setting up the agent's instructions with a structured research workflow that includes web searching, page fetching, note-taking, and final summarization. Then I'm determining the research topic from command-line arguments or defaulting to quantum computing advances, and starting the research process with a console message.

Now I'm configuring the agent session with specific chat options like zero temperature for deterministic output and a token limit, then streaming the research results to the console as the agent works through the topic systematically.
